title: Training Coordination Solutions
description: A 16-month co-op project with the Ministry of Transportation's Engagement and Advisory Branch — creating a digital registration and tracking system for employee training courses.
challenge: How might we help training coordinators by reducing the time-consuming, complex, and manual nature of their current workflow?
backgroundInfo:
The Training Coordination Solutions project was developed to address the growing complexity of managing employee training across the Ontario Ministry of Transportation's Engagement and Advisory Branch. Training coordinators were responsible for organizing, registering, and tracking dozens of courses across the branch — all while managing multiple disconnected systems and fielding requests through email.

The ministry's existing process placed coordinators at the centre of every action: employees couldn't register themselves, managers had to relay requests, and there was no single source of truth for tracking who was enrolled in what. This created a significant administrative burden and introduced frequent errors and delays.

The goal of this project was to design a digital system that would streamline the end-to-end training workflow — empowering employees to self-register, reducing the coordinator's manual workload, and giving managers clear visibility into their team's training progress.
insights:
During the discovery phase, we conducted workshops and semi-structured interviews with the three core user groups: Business Service Analysts (BSAs) acting as training coordinators, managers who relay training requests, and employees who attend courses.

Key insights that shaped the design direction:

Coordinators spent a disproportionate amount of time on manual data entry and status chasing — tasks that could be automated or delegated. Employees had no direct visibility into what courses were available or when they were offered. Managers acted as unnecessary middlemen, forwarding requests they had no capacity to track. LearnON, the existing platform, was poorly adopted because it didn't reflect the branch's actual workflow.

These findings led us to redesign the registration model around employee self-service, with coordinators shifting into an oversight role rather than an operational one.
role:
User Research — Led discovery workshops and interviews with Business Service Analysts (BSAs), managers, and employees.

UX Design — Designed prototypes for three distinct user types: training coordinator, employee, and manager.

Usability Testing — Facilitated three testing phases with BSAs and managers, iterating on feedback each round.

Process Improvement — Co-created a streamlined, employee-driven registration model to reduce coordinator workload.

prototypes.title: Manage Courses (Training Coordinator View)
prototypes.caption: A dashboard allowing coordinators to create, manage, and track training courses across the branch. Coordinators can view enrollment numbers, manage waitlists, and export records — all from a single interface.

prototypes.title: Find & Register for Courses (Employee View)
prototypes.caption: An employee-facing course catalogue that lets staff browse available sessions, view details, and self-register without involving a manager or coordinator. Registration status is tracked in real time.

prototypes.title: View Employee Training (Manager View)
prototypes.caption: A read-only reporting view that gives managers clear visibility into their team's training progress, upcoming sessions, and completion records — without becoming a bottleneck in the registration process.

title: Highway Corridor Management System
description: A live-phase contribution to the Ontario Ministry of Transportation's system for tracking and managing road work on or near highways across Ontario — May to August 2025.
backgroundInfo:
The Highway Corridor Management System (HCMS) was built to make it easier for the Ontario Ministry of Transportation to manage construction and development near highways. Before HCMS, applications and reviews were handled across emails, spreadsheets, and separate systems — causing confusion and delays.

The new system brings everything into one place, making the process smoother, faster, and easier to track. It also helps ensure that all projects comply with safety standards and planning requirements.
role:
Wireframes — Designed and updated screens based on client feedback across both desktop and mobile views.

Workshops — Presented ideas and walked developers and clients through designs in collaborative working sessions.

User Stories — Worked through DevOps to gather and post information onto wireframes to support development handoff.

prototypes.title: Update a Submission
prototypes.caption: One of 9 screens created to update and track a submission. These pages are used to upload files relating to each permit. Every permit has a unique number for tracking.

prototypes.title: Application Database
prototypes.caption: A master view used to keep track of all permits and their associated information. Users can click an application number to open the full permit detail page.

prototypes.title: Manage Payment of Permits
prototypes.caption: A receipt page that appears after a transaction within a permit. Displays all relevant information and the total paid. Names are blurred and other details use placeholders.

title: The Citizen POA Court Experience
description: A semester-long group project to redesign the Brantford Provincial Offences Court — improving its atmosphere, navigation, and accessibility as it relocated to a new building.
challenge: How might we redesign the courthouse experience to create a more welcoming, accessible, and supportive environment that reduces confusion, frustration, and anxiety for first-time and returning visitors?
backgroundInfo: Our team partnered with the City of Brantford to redesign the citizen’s courthouse experience. Our design proposal aims to create a space which is more welcoming, intuitive, and accessible to citizens, both new to the system and repeated visitors. Our objective was to help provide a more comfortable and understandable experience while providing the support systems needed to help citizens reach a successful outcome from their court experience. Over 4 months, we researched, designed, and tested the space used by experienced courthouse visitors. The project consisted of several design iterations, research, and testing deliverables to keep the process on track and gain feedback.
role:
​​​​​​​Research  - Created surveys to understand feeling and usability testing to understand the users journey 

Design  - Created wireframes and the 3d model for the court space

Communication - Lead communicator with users with sending out emails and setting up meetings

prototypes.title: First Time Courtroom Experience
prototypes.caption: Wireframe of the initial onboarding flow guiding citizens through what to expect on their first visit to a POA courtroom.

prototypes.title: Last Time Courtroom Experience
prototypes.caption: Prototype showing the end-of-process flow, including resolution screens and next-step guidance for citizens completing their court experience.

prototypes.title: Final Prototype
prototypes.caption: High-fidelity prototype consolidating research findings into a refined experience across all key citizen touchpoints.

extraSections.heading: Final Design
extraSections.body:
Atmosphere — Warm-toned wood for walls and furniture, with cooler blue accents for balance — combining welcome with professionalism.

Navigation & Wayfinding — Clear signage and information boards throughout, with a logical linear layout guiding citizens from entry to courtroom.

Accessibility — Improved seating accessibility throughout the space, with accessible bathrooms positioned near the lobby.

Proposed Services — Mediation services, legal assistance, and victim support — though full exploration was limited by delays in receiving client documentation.

extraSections.heading: Reflection
extraSections.body: Although the project was successful overall, we weren't able to fully explore adding new services for courthouse visitors due to delays in receiving client documentation. This reinforced the importance of establishing clear lines of communication and access to project constraints early in the design process.

designProcess.title: Research
designProcess.desc: Conducted surveys, interviews, and observational studies with first-time and repeat courthouse visitors.

designProcess.title: Analysis
designProcess.desc: Synthesized findings into personas, journey maps, and key insight themes to guide design decisions.

designProcess.title: Design
designProcess.desc: Created wireframes, spatial layouts, and a 3D model of the proposed courthouse experience.

designProcess.title: Testing
designProcess.desc: Ran usability testing sessions and iterated the design based on structured participant feedback.

researchMethods.body:
To understand the citizen experience, our team conducted surveys, contextual interviews, and observational studies at the existing courthouse. We engaged with both first-time and repeat visitors to identify common pain points, emotional responses, and navigational challenges.

Our research revealed that citizens frequently felt lost, anxious, and unsupported upon entering the courthouse. Lack of clear wayfinding, limited seating, and an absence of visible support services were the primary drivers of a negative experience.

analysisResults.body:
Insights from our research were synthesized into personas and journey maps representing distinct citizen archetypes — the first-time offender, the repeat visitor, and the support-seeker.

Key themes emerged around three areas: environmental anxiety driven by an unfamiliar and formal atmosphere, navigational confusion due to poor signage and layout, and a lack of access to legal and emotional support services.

title: Maker Lab II – SDP2.2
description: A wearable alert bracelet designed to give deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals the freedom to move around their home without worrying about missing an important alarm.
backgroundInfo:
The project addresses accessibility for deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals by creating a wearable alert device. The bracelet provides visual, auditory, and haptic (vibration) feedback when an alarm sounds — giving users the freedom to move around their home without worrying about missing something important.

The prototype successfully produced a sound with a light vibration when something was within a certain distance, though it didn't fully integrate all intended features due to challenges with component integration and finding the appropriate code.

extraSections.heading: Design & Build
extraSections.body:
Components used: Arduino Uno, breadboard, LED, wires, resistors, sensors, and a sound machine. The team encountered challenges in finding appropriate code for all components and struggled with integration between hardware elements.

Alert Feedback Modes:
- Visual — LED light response
- Auditory — sound output
- Haptic — vibration feedback

extraSections.heading: Future Vision
extraSections.body: An interactive app that allows users to connect their devices — such as their fire alarm and doorbell — to the customized bracelet. Despite the unmet initial goals, the project was a valued learning experience in hardware prototyping, accessibility design, and collaborative iteration.

title: One Guard
description: A physical keyboard guard designed to help people with physical or cognitive disabilities learn and use the keyboard — making the experience comfortable for everyone.
backgroundInfo: The One Guard is a way for people to easily use the keyboard. Whether you have a physical, cognitive, or no disability — the One Guard is meant to make your experience with the keyboard nice and easy.

extraSections.heading: Design Process
extraSections.body:
The team initially used cardboard for prototyping but found it wasn't accurate and was too flimsy to be practical. They pivoted to hot glue as the primary structural material, with additional components to improve accessibility and usability.

Materials:
- Hot glue — primary structural material
- Zip ties — to attach the guard to the keyboard
- Zip lock bags — to help attach the braille elements
- Little gems — used as braille indicators
- Stickers — to add colour coding to the keys

extraSections.heading: Reflection
extraSections.body: In the end, the team was proud of what was accomplished. This project helped illustrate what it's like to work in a group where everyone brings their own skills toward a common goal — particularly when that goal centres around meaningful accessibility outcomes.
